coredata.dc:title: Development of fluorescent carbon dots ink from rice straw waste toward security authentication
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Qahtani, Salhah D.
Hameed, Ahmed
Snari, Razan M.
Shah, Reem
Abdulaziz Alfi, Alia
shaaban, Fathy
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: New photochromic emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanocomposite ink was prepared for anticounterfeiting digital printing. We developed a simple, green and effective approach to reuse rice straw waste as a precursor for the preparation of fluorescent N-CDs via one-pot hydro-thermal carbonization in an aquatic medium using NH4OH as a cheap passivating substance. N-doped carbon dots displayed QY (quantum yield) up to 22.51% with an excitation of 356 nm and fluorescence maxima at 452 nm. The photochromic emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots nanocomposite ink was digitally printed onto cellulose sheets demonstrating a colorless appearance with a capability to change color to blue emission underneath UV as verified by CIE Lab. N-CDs was prepared in the nanoparticle form and studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The morphologies of the printed sheets were studied by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and FT-IR. Under ultraviolet and visible lights, the printed sheets showed an effective reversible fluorescence photochromism without fatigue. The rheological performance of the prepared N-CDs nanocomposite ink was explored. The mechanical behavior of the printed sheets was also investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rice straw
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Security ink
Documents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of N-doped CDs based fluorescent ink
Preparation and characterization of N-doped carbon dots
Application of N-CDs onto paper sheets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of NH4OH mass ratio to cellulose diacetate on QY of N-CDs.
XRD spectra of CDA (a), and N-CDs (b).
FT-IR spectral analysis of CDA (a), and N-CDs (b).
Excitation (a) and fluorescence (b) spectral analysis of N-doped carbon dots in aqueous medium; and photos of N-CDs beneath daylight (c) and ultraviolet light (d).
Fluorescence spectral analysis of N-doped carbon dots at various pH values.
Msc viability as a function of N-doped carbon dots concentration.
Viscosity of ink versus shear rate.
Pre-designed pattern printed onto an off-white cellulose paper sheet (left) demonstrating blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (right).
Reversibility of emission intensity at 452 nm (N-CDs6).
Effect of N-CDs ratio on mechanical properties.
SEM images of N-CDs1 (a, b), and N-CDs8 (c, d).
Diagram representing the preparation procedure of nitrogen-doped carbon dots.
Coloration screening of printed sheets at various N-CDs ratios under sunlight (SL) and ultraviolet irradiation (UVI).
Elements ratios of N-CDs0 and printed sheet (N-CDs6) at three sites of the paper surface.

head.ce:title: Development of fluorescent carbon dots ink from rice straw waste toward security authentication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Mansoura University, El-Gomhoria Street 35516, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Applied Science, Umm-Al-Qura University, Makkah 24230, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New photochromic emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanocomposite ink was prepared for anticounterfeiting digital printing. We developed a simple, green and effective approach to reuse rice straw waste as a precursor for the preparation of fluorescent N-CDs via one-pot hydro-thermal carbonization in an aquatic medium using NH4OH as a cheap passivating substance. N-doped carbon dots displayed QY (quantum yield) up to 22.51% with an excitation of 356 nm and fluorescence maxima at 452 nm. The photochromic emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots nanocomposite ink was digitally printed onto cellulose sheets demonstrating a colorless appearance with a capability to change color to blue emission underneath UV as verified by CIE Lab. N-CDs was prepared in the nanoparticle form and studied by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The morphologies of the printed sheets were studied by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and FT-IR. Under ultraviolet and visible lights, the printed sheets showed an effective reversible fluorescence photochromism without fatigue. The rheological performance of the prepared N-CDs nanocomposite ink was explored. The mechanical behavior of the printed sheets was also investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been a global issue with the forgery of commercial products such as documents, foodstuff, and banknotes that has tremendous economic and security consequences for the industrial sector, consumers and the governments. Research into new anti-counterfeiting materials has gotten under way due to the pressing need to create a new generation of sophisticated counterfeit detection systems [1]. The anti-counterfeiting optical systems have been a common security method since it is simple to read but difficult to copy. Dispersion of fluorescent materials in the ink formulation method could be used to establish a security mark on commercial goods. Anti-counterfeiting materials including fluorescent and plasmonic nanoparticles, have been successfully used in a variety of optical functional systems. A resin containing organic fluorescent colorants can be used in printing to create security inks [2,3]. However, organic fluorescent dyes often encounter steric effects when enclosed in a printed film matrix. Moreover, the ultraviolet light exposure usually causes deterioration and a progressive decline in the fluorescence efficacy of organic dyes, limiting their usage outdoors or under UV sources [4]. Nanomaterials have been known for their ability to maintain transparency of prints [5,6]. Photoluminescent nanomaterials underwent much development for applications in information encryption and security authentication. Among these luminescent nanomaterials, carbon dots were interesting nanoparticles that were found to exhibit low toxicity, biocompatibility, and outstanding optical properties while being easy to prepare [7]. These features make them appealing replacements for organic dyes. They are also strong contenders of semiconductor-based quantum dots which suffer low quantum yields, complicated synthesis methods, high cost, and high toxicity. Carbon dots are proposed for a wide range of applications including item packaging and information encoding, anti-counterfeiting, cellular labeling and imaging, switch devices development, and substance and explosives detection [8–14]. Carbon dots are nanoparticles roughly spherical in shape with a diameter in the tens of nanometers or less. They can be prepared either by a top-down method, starting with a bulk of carbon materials, or a bottom-up procedure, starting with precursor seeds to initiate nanoparticle formation. In the later route, carbon dots are commonly fabricated from citric acid, amino acids, and organic polymers, such as carbohydrates and other biomasses. Fluorescent carbon dots have been synthesized with different methods resulting in varying quantum yields [15]. Sk and Chattopadhyay produced uniform sized carbon dots with a 73.5% florescent quantum yield using citric acid as a starting material heated at 100 and 130 °C on a commercial induction hotplate for no more than 15 min [16]. Liu et al. reported a 75% quantum yield from nitrogen doped carbon dots prepared from citric acid using electrothermal processes with temperatures between 25 °C and 95 °C [17]. Nitrogen doping enhances both the photo and thermal stability of carbon dots. It is reported that nitrogen doped carbon dots have shifted emissions of bright blue florescence unlike the green florescence of carbon-based quantum dots. Blue shift emission is also reported as pH-dependent. The mechanism may be associated with carbon dots aggregations or protonation, but there is no definite explanation of how red or blue shifts happen. Khan et al. reported a 46.4% quantum yield from green emitting carbon dots prepared form urea and diammonium hydrogen citrate via a solid state reaction approach [18]. In addition, Li et al. reported nitrogen doped carbon dots emitting green at room temperature after UV light cessation [19]. They were prepared form citric acid, urea, and acrylamide as staring materials. Furthermore, Jiang et al. reported multiple color carbon dots for both blue to green florescence and cyan to yellow phosphorescent emissions with on and off UV modes, respectively [20]. Fluorescence is simply defined as a light emission by a certain compound due to previous absorption of light [2]. On the other hand, photochromism is a light induced reversible color change [3]. The features of having multiple emission wavelengths from fluorescence to phosphorescence can be used for developing strong and confident anti-counterfeiting recognition applications. Recently, photochromic bio-based carbon dots have been synthesized from plants [21]. Gao’s group showed that nitrogen doped carbon dots obtained from willow leaves exhibited intense blue fluorescence under a 356 nm UV light. Their fluorescent photo stability, damped at a decay rate of 0.02%, is superior to their dye counterpart such as rhodamine, damped at a rate of 0.45% [22].

The aim of the current paper is to develop a simple and green strategy for the preparation of high quality carbon dots from rice straw waste. Fluorescence photochromism has been used as an effective dual-mode security encoding method for the development of efficient authentication materials. Rice straw wastes have been a global growing problem. Thus, finding simple and appropriate technologies to convert rice straw into value-added products is of high importance. Herein, a one-pot hydrothermal approach with the advantages of cheapness, environmental friendliness, and large scale production was used to produce carbon dots. Rather of employing a costly and toxic amine-terminated passivation agent, NH4OH was used as a passivating agent to insert the nitrogen element onto the surfaces of the carbon dots to enhance the quantum yield. In order to develop smart fluorescent anti-counterfeiting security ink, we described the preparation and application of fluorescent N-CDs ink onto paper sheets by printing. For its fluorescent security performance and printability, we created and analyzed the printed fluorescence security inks. The paper-based printed fluorescence security ink was created by combining N-CDs and resin along with other additives. Fluorescent security ink printed onto paper documents was tested for optical performance using CIE Lab color coordinates, excitation and emission spectra. TEM was utilized to examine the morphological features of the prepared N-CDs, while SEM and FTIR were used to examine the morphological properties of the printed paper sheets. Stability and printability of the ink were also examined. Because of their large quantum yield and intense fluorescence, an “on-off” fluorescence ink based on the generated N-containing carbon dots can be used as an authentication encoding against counterfeiting and forgery of documents.

The chemicals used in the current study, including NH4OH (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt), NaClO2 (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt), H2SO4 (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt), HCl (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt), DMSO (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt), and NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich; Egypt) were purchased from commercial sources. Acetic anhydride and tetrabutylammonium acetate (TBAA) were obtained from Merck (Egypt). Wahttman (off-white) cellulose sheets with a pore size of 11 μm, thickness of 180 μm, weight of 87 g/m2 and diameter of 240 mm were provided from Merck (Egypt). The de-ionized water was employed all through the entire experimental work.

Rice straws were provided from Sharqia Governorate, Egypt. Cellulose diacetate was prepared from rice straw according to previously reported procedures [23,24]. Rice straws of about 5 cm in length were immersed for 2 h in a 17.5 wt% sodium hydroxide solution. The swollen straws were then rinsed and blended for around half an hour in a blender before being air-dried under ambient conditions. The intermolecular connections in plant cell walls are broken by the entrance of sodium hydroxide into the amorphous zone. To eliminate hemicelluloses, extractive components, and the amorphous portions of cellulose, a diluted hydrochloric solution was used to hydrolyze the resulting bulk. A constant stirrer speed was used to execute hydrolysis treatments at 80 °C with varying acid contents (1, 2 and 3 mol) and periods (1, 2, and 3 h). With distilled water and air drying, the pH of the bulk was brought back to a neutral state. The hydrolyzed bulk was exposed to NaOH(aq) (2% w/v) for 2 h at 80 °C with a steady stirring speed, and then washed and dried. After treating with alkali, the sample was still brown due to the presence of the insoluble lignin. Consequently, the sample was subjected to treatment with NaClO2(aq) (20% w/v) at 50 °C for an hour to complete the bleaching process. The fibers kappa number was used to estimate the quantity of sodium chlorite needed. Once the fibers had been bleached, they were rinsed and air-dried. A 125 mL of distilled water was used to create the chemically pure cellulose fibre slurry (1% wt), which was then homogenized (20 KHz; 400 W) for 30 min using a cylindrical probe with a diameter of 1.5 cm to fibrillate nanofibers. The homogenization process was done in an ice bath to regulate the temperature. Cellulose diacetate (CDA) with a degree of substitution (DS) of 2.62 was prepared as previously reported [24]. The cellulose fibers extracted from rice straw was gradually added into a solvent mixture of DMSO and TBAA (8:2). The mixture was exposed to stirring at 60 °C for 30 min until obtaining a colorless cellulose solution (8 wt%). Acetic anhydride was then slowly added to mixture. The solution was subjected to stirring for 3 h at 60 °C, and then exposed to precipitation in methanol. CDA was produced after filtration, rinsing with methanol, and finally drying under vacuum.

According to previously reported procedures [25], the hydrothermal technique was used to create N-CDs. It was necessary to chop up and dry the rice straw extract first at 80 °C for an hour after they had been washed numerous times with water. Ammonium hydroxide solution was added to 0.2 g of the prepared rice straw extract, and the mixtures were subjected to dilution to 20 mL using de-ionized water. A Teflon-lined autoclave, with a 50 mL capacity, was used to heat the combinations for 15 h at 240 °C. Cooling the autoclave to normal temperature was done when the reaction was complete. To remove the dark precipitates, the admixtures were centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 10 min. Impurities were removed by dialyzing the filtrate against de-ionized water in a dialysis tubing (MWCO 1000) over 24 h after it had been purified by filtration with an ultra-fine 0.22 μm filter. The produced N-activated carbon dots were kept at 4 °C for future investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-doped CDs based fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Different solutions were prepared by dispersing different concentrations of N-CDs (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700 and 800 mg) in ultrapure water (100 mL). The provided inks were represented by N-CDs1, N-CDs2, N-CDs3, N-CDs4, N-CDs5, N-CDs6, N-CDs7, and N-CDs8, respectively. Each solution was applied directly as an anti-counterfeiting printing ink. Each ink was injected into an empty cartridge of ink-jet printer (EPSON desk-jet L310). Pre-designed patterns were printed onto an unbleached Wahttman cellulose paper sheets using a conventional printer attached to the computer. Photos (Canon A710IS camera) of the printed papers were taken under UV lamp (6 W) at 365 nm.

To acquire all of the photoluminescence spectra, JASCO FP-6500 (Tokyo, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer was used. UV-1006-M-031 spectrophotometer (Shanghai Varian Spectrum Analysis instrument Co., Ltd.) was utilized to determine the absorbance spectra of the carbon dots. N-CDs and printed paper sheets were analyzed using Nexus 670 spectrophotometer for FT-IR spectra (Nicolet, United States). With the use of Quanta FEG-250 SEM (Republic of Czech) paired to EDXA, the printed paper sheets morphological characteristics and chemical compositions were examined. The printed sheets' tensile strength, strain %, and Young's modulus were reported using Zwick Universal (Zwick GmbH & Co, Ulm, Germany). For each sample, an average of five readings was provided. Using the Brookfield DV-III Rheometer, the rheological characteristics were analyzed. The apparent viscosity of the produced samples was measured using rheological techniques. XRD spectra of CDA and N-doped were studied by Bruker Advance D8 (Bruker; Germany). TEM (JEOL1230; JEOL Co. Ltd. Japan) was used to examine both shape and size of N-CDs. The following equation was used to calculate QY of N-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm utilizing quinine sulfate standards with a quantum yield of 54% in H2SO4 solution (0.1 M) [26].
(1)
Q Y =
( I /
I r
)
∗
(
A r
/ A )
∗
(
η
2
/
η r
2
)
where QY is the quantum yield; η is the solvent refractive index; r represents standard samples; I is the fluorescence intensity; and A is the absorption intensity.

For testing the N-CDs cell toxicity, a cell counting kit-8 (Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt) assay was used. DMEM (Dulbecco's modified eagle medium; Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt) was used to cultivate mouse skin cells (Msc; Sigma-Aldrich, Egypt) on a 96-well plate for 24 h at 24.2 °C in an incubation system with CO2 (5 %). Once N-CDs had been added to the DMEM, the cells were subjected to incubation for an additional 24 h at 24.3 °C and CO2 (5 %) to see whether they had any effect on the cells. The DMEM medium was replaced with N-doped CDs at different contents (0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 μg/mL). The cells were subjected to incubation for an additional 24 hurs at 24.3 °C with 5% CO2. CCK-8 was added to the well after the DMEM solution was withdrawn, and the well was then subjected to incubation for 4 h at 37 °C with CO2 (5 %). At a wavelength of 490 nm, the cell's optical density was finally determined. The Msc that were not infected with N-CDs were utilized as controls since they were incubated in the same way. The vitality of mouse skin cells was utilized to represent cytotoxicity, and the equation below was used to arrive at that conclusion [27–31].
(2)
M s c v i a b i l i t y p e r c e n t = (
N D
/
N T
)
∗
100
where NT is the control sample absorbance; and ND is the Msc cells with different ratios of N-doped CDs.

An UV lamp (365 nm and 6 W) was utilized to excite the printed sheet for 5 min and then left in the daylight for 5 min. The sample was placed 5 cm above the UV lamp's beam path. Repeated exposure to UV rays and subsequent fading was used. The spectra of the emitted radiation were recorded after each cycle.

Ultra Scan Pro (HunterLab; United States) was used to record coloration data (CIE Lab) and color strength (K/S) before and after exposing to ultraviolet radiation. CIE is the abbreviation of the French name, Commission Internationale de L'éclairage or known by the International Commission on Illumination [32]. Three-dimensional color coordinates, include L* (0 for blackness to 100 for dazzling whiteness), a* (red for + a* and green for -a*), and b* (yellowness for + b* and blue for -b*), were examined. Samples were irradiated for 2 min with an UV light (365 nm) to analyze coloring measures. The sample was positioned 4 cm below the ultraviolet light. The UV supply was turned off and the colorimetric data were immediately recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrothermal treatment at 240 °C for 15 hrs resulted in the formation of N-CDs starting from rice straw waste as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Rice straw was soaked in NaOH(aq), and then exposed to hydrolysis using a diluted hydrochloric solution. The cellulose fibers extracted from rice straw was subjected to treatment with a mixture of DMSO and TBAA, and then acetic anhydride to provide CDA. As a reaction medium and passivation agent, the dilute ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution was used instead of the more costly and poisonous organic passivation agents, which often included several terminal amine groups in high concentrations. Ammonium hydroxide mass ratio to CDA was tested to determine the best QY of N-CDs as displayed in Fig. 1 . The ammonium hydroxide to CDA mass ratio was adjusted from 1:0 to 1:10 in order to raise the QY from 8.25 to 22.51%. QY of N-CDs would decline further if the mass ratio was increased to 1:15. For this reason, ammonium hydroxide and CDA mass ratios were tinkered with to produce N-CDs with QY of up to 22.51%, which was in line with other values of biomass precursors. Preceding studies on the degradation and carbonization of cellulose suggested that N-CDs could be formed by the hydrolysis of cellulose diacetate in the initial stage, and then condensation of short fragments to yield nitrogen doped carbon dots. Glycosidic bonds connect glucose-like units in CDA, a cellulose derivative [33]. Glucose, fructose, acetic acid, and other intermediate CDA fragments could be formed in NH4OH(aq) by breaking of the glycosidic link and deacetylation of CDA [25]. Ammonium hydroxide is also thought to have formed new types of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups. In the subsequent hydrothermal treatment, the previously stated cellulose diacetate fragments were subjected to further condensation and carbonization to produce the final carbon structure of the nitrogen doped carbon dots [34]. The nucleophilic substitution of NH3 to carbon's hydroxyls on the N-CD surface was the key to successfully passivating nitrogen on the N-CD surface [35]. The nitrogen doped carbon dots were efficiently dispersed spherical nanoparticles. There was a wide variety of sizes for N-CDs in the TEM images, with diameters of ∼45 nm as shown in Fig. 2 . Previous studies have reported that N-CDs had no lattice structure [34,35]. Fig. 3 shows that in the XRD pattern of CDA, two broad peaks were seen at 8.3° and 19.1°, which are attributable to the CDA's characteristic crystallization phase, while the carbon dots only exhibited a broad band at 22.8°, which is due to the carbons amorphous phase. FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 4
) were also utilized to recognize the N-CDs surface functional substituents. When N-CDs were studied by FT-IR, the spectra showed that the NH/OH stretch vibrations at 3284 cm−1 were accompanied by stretch vibrations at 1258 and 786 cm−1, respectively. N-doped CDs showed these peaks, indicating a successful N-doping process. The existence of SO3/C-O stretch vibration at 1007 cm−1 suggested the existence of sulfur in the nitrogen doped carbon dots [25].

As a new sort of nano-scaled structures, CDs with dimensions lower than 100 nm, have received high interest. Carbon dots have displayed excellent emission properties and offered distinctive advantages over other fluorescent materials, such as low cost, non-toxicity, biocompatibility [7–9]. Carbon dots are described as tiny molecular entities or polymers generated upon condensation, cross-linking and carbonization. Thus, carbon dots usually exhibit the structure of both inorganic carbon core and fluorescent polymers. This distinctive structure supports the CDs with excellent fluorescence QY and excellent water solubility. Various studies have been reported for the development of CDs with the desired potential applications, such as bioimaging, sensing and chemical catalysis [7–15]. Among many other approaches, the hydrothermal curing of biomass precursors, such as cellulose, chitosan, starch, hemicellulose and lignin, has received great attention owing to their renewability, cheapness and green characteristics. However, those biomass precursors commonly have demonstrated high value as resources in various industrial fields. Thus, the use of those biomass waste precursors to produce high-quality carbon dots as an alternative to the valuable industrial materials remains urgently desirable [34,35]. In this study, we choose a cost-effective and eco-friendly strategy to develop a fluorescent carbon dots-based film from rice-straw waste. Rice-straw is an abundant biomass that is rich in cellulous. Cellulous was proved a suitable starting material for carbon dots preparation [36–38]. Nitrogen doped carbon dots were produced via simple hydrothermal treatment in the presence of a nitrogen passivating agent. The florescent carbon dots were coupled to a transparent polymer to produce a carbon dots gel that is then converted to film. This composite film was tested for its photochromic dual mode emission under visible and UV light. Blue emission was tested on a turn-on UV mode [7,8]. The advent of synthetic photoluminescent carbon dots has exciting potentials. The simple method of carbon dots synthesis makes dual mode fluorescent emission inks production at a large-scale possible. Utilizing abundant agricultural waste for the synthesis of fluorescence carbon dots can serve a broader range of florescent based applications [39]. The optical characteristics of the as-prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots were exceptional. Under daylight conditions, the N-CDs aqueous solution appeared yellow, but when irradiated with an ultraviolet source at 365 nm, it produced a blue luminescence. Two peaks at 275 and 325 nm in the nitrogen-doped carbon dots' absorbance spectra were ascribed to carbon–carbon bonding (π-π* transitions) and carbon–oxygen bonding (n-π* transition). The excitation of 356 nm was found to be ideal for N-CDs fluorescence at 452 nm. The QY was found to be 22.51%. The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were also red-shifted and their intensity decreased once the excitation was increased from 325 to 356 nm with an increase of 12 nm. There have been several reports of this excitation-dependent fluorescence activity [40]. An investigation was conducted to determine how pH and irradiation time affected the emission stability of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots in water. The findings of the emission spectral analysis are presented in Figs. 5 and 6
. Using a pH range of 4–7, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots showed outstanding fluorescence stability in mild acidic aqueous environments. The emission intensities of N-CDs were highest in solutions with a pH of 5. The reduced emission intensity of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots was shown to be a result of protonation-deprotonation process of functional substituents on the N-CDs surface when pH was increased from 7 to 13 or decreased from 4 to 1. The N-CDs were thus thought to be inert in the somewhat acidic setting. As pH increasing from 1 to 13, the fluorescence band of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots progressively blue-shifted. Such performance was also monitored in formerly published research work, such as graphene oxide based quantum dots [41], and N-CDs prepared from citric acid [42], and cellulose [43]. Based on prior research, certain plausible pH-responsive mechanism models, such as self-aggregation, energy level shift, protonation-deprotonation, carbon dot aggregation and protective shell, have been presented [44]. Carbon dots research is still in its infancy, so a specific mechanism for this behavior has yet to be discovered and approved [45]. For example, no accurate structure of CDs has been verified, limiting the ability to analyze the structure-optical relationship with greater precision. Because carbon dots could display distinct pH-dependent emission even if they were generated using the same precursors or using the same processes, it was difficult to draw conclusions. Red-shifted emission was reported by Yuan for carbon dots made from graphite rods [46], whereas Fan produced CDs utilizing the identical precursors but with a blue-shifting activity [47]. For the carbon dots, Liu [48] and Barati [49] produced them from citric acid and glucose using the same hydrothermal process. These two forms of carbon dots, on the other hand, have differing pH-dependent emission characteristics. Moreover, the N-CDs showed exceptional photostability, as shown by their hardly altered fluorescence intensities when irradiated for 10–120 min. This was presumably ascribed to the carboxyl substituents on N-CDs surface.

MTT assays [28–31,50–52] were used to test the toxicity of N-CDs against MSc in DMEM with varying doses of N-CDs added for 24 h; the results are shown in Fig. 7 . N-CDs concentrations up to 150 g/mL were shown to have no effect on the viability of Msc cells. Thus, the N-CDs were shown to be safe for usage on healthy cell lines, and this suggests that they might be used as an optical probe for various anti-counterfeiting applications such as food packaging.

As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the rheological characteristics of the fluorescent security ink were examined. As its flowing curve did not conform to undergo Newton's equations, the formed ink was slightly viscous, and it was discovered that the apparent viscosity decreased with increasing external shear, with the viscosity and shear rate showing a non-linear correlation. It was discovered that the sample's apparent viscosity decreased rapidly at lower shear rates while it decreased slowly and steadily toward equilibrium at higher shear rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs onto paper sheets

ce:section.ce:para: The coloration results were used to test the ability of the printed documents to change color. It was determined that the photo-switchable printed ink's technical performance, including reversibility and photostability, was also investigated using CIE Lab color screening as illustrated in Table 1 . The pristine and treated sheets had an off-white shade to indicate a transparent appearance of the printed film. Under both daylight and UV, K/S increased, indicating that the tinctorial strength had strengthened with increasing the N-CDs ratio. However, K/S slightly increased with increasing the N-CDs ratio under daylight to indicate the formation of a faintly yellow color, and significantly increased with increasing the N-CDs ratio under UV light to indicate the formation of a darker blue color. Under daylight, the L*, a* and b* of the printed sheets did not differ significantly from those of the off-white blank sheet when the N-CDs total content was increased. As a result of the ink matrix's low N-CDs concentration, no differences in K/S of the off-white sheets were observed under daylight before or after printing, showing its transparency as a photochromic layer printed onto the sheet surface. Results showed that the best coloring measures were achieved at a total N-CDs concentration of 600 mg. The photo-switchable printed layer's excitation and emission intensities increased when treated sheets were irradiated with UV. With increasing the ratio of N-CDs, both −a* and +b* demonstrated negligible changes under daylight, while L* slightly decreased. This indicates that the printed paper sheets were coated with a colorless ink film. While −a* and −b* increased beneath UV rays, L* decreased. This indicates that the printed paper sheets had a color shift to blue. Thus, the off-white color of the coated paper sheets became blue when exposed to UV radiation.

Fig. 10 shows the results of testing the N-CDs security ink printed sheet for fatigue resistance under various conditions of daylight and ultraviolet exposure. In terms of fatigue resistance, the fluorescent film (N-CDs6) performed well. In order for the luminous printed film to revert to its original fading condition, it was exposed to UV light for 5 min and then left under daylight for 5 min. The emission intensity observed after each UV irradiation and fading cycle was virtually the same as the intensity recorded after the initial ultraviolet irradiation, indicating great durability, photostability, and reversibility of the fluorescent layer.

To inspect the mechanical performance, the sheets were sprayed with the N-CDs ink [53,54]. Fig. 11 illustrates Young's modulus, tensile and strain percentage at break for the printed paper sheets at various total N-CDs concentrations. When the pigment concentration was raised from N-CDs0 to N-CDs6, both tensile and Young's modulus increased, but only slight changes were detected for the concentrations from N-CDs6 to N-CDs8. When increasing the N-CDs content from N-CDs0 to N-CDs8, very minor variations were seen in the percentage of strain at break.

Although rice is an important source of food for many people around the world, it produces a lot of straw and husks each year, with an estimated annual production of around 8 × 1011 kg of straw and 1.5 × 1011 kg of husks, respectively [55]. Most rice straw is burnt in situ, integrated into the soil, or mulched for the next crop, with just around 20% now being utilized for convenient uses including the manufacture of fertilizers, paper, animal feeding and biofuel [56–58]. However, rice straw buried in the soil has been reported to slowly degrade and can potentially cause rice disease. It has been more improper to burn rice straw due to the significant contamination of the atmosphere caused by greenhouse gas emissions, as well as the smoke that comes with it. Moraes et al. have analyzed the components generated from the rice production cycle [59]. Research works have been reported to discover economically and socially suitable applications for agriculture wastes. Even though broken grains and their bran can be used as functional foods, rice bran does have potential applications as functional foods because of its capability to inhibit the formation of Salmonella colony in the gastrointestinal tracts. It is also an oil source with a variety of beneficial characteristics for humans and animals [60]. Abraham et al. have also looked at the possible utilization of rice straw in the manufacture of biofuel and other products [61]. Due to the fact that this waste is generated off site, a larger proportion of it has been put to use, despite the fact that rice husks were previously deemed garbage and generally abandoned or burnt. Despite this, rice straw has long been used in small-scale energy production due to its accessibility and low cost. In addition, a variety of rice straw products were developed in recent years, such as polymer composite resin and polymer lumber as an alternative to natural woods, both of which are made by mixing grinded rice straw with polymeric resins [36–38].

Products having smart characteristics are able to react to external stimuli providing innovative changeable characteristics [62–64]. Fluorescent inks are examples of smart materials that change color when exposed to light. Anti-counterfeiting papers based on printing technology have lately gained greater attention because of its customizable structure and prospective use in several domains, such as biological imaging, logical gates, sensors, and security warnings. As shown in Fig. 12 , SEM images were utilized to inspect the morphological characteristics of both blank and luminous films. The N-CDs were successfully printed onto the paper sheet as proved by SEM inages, which demonstrated a thin coating of the nano-scale nitrogen-doped carbon dots. These images proved that the printing deposition technique was able to successfully distribute N-CDs onto the surface of the cellulose sheet. An even dispersion of the N-CDs onto the cellulose surface was observed because of the N-CDs's nano-scaled structure. SEM images showed no physical differences in the paper sheets cellulose fibrous structure of both treated and untreated document surfaces. EDX was utilized to inspect the elemental contents of N-CDs6 at various locations on the treated sheet surface as shown in Table 2 . Documents printed with pigment were found to have an essentially same elemental composition when scanned at three different points. Doping success was further confirmed by finding larger levels of sulfur and nitrogen in N-CDs than in the CDA, with levels at 1.13% and 14.98%, respectively, above the CDA's levels. No further sulfur-containing compounds, except for cellulose diacetate, were employed throughout the entire process of making N-doped CDs, whereas nitrogen was predominantly obtained from the passivating agent (ammonium hydroxide), whereas CDA was monitored as the carbon source.

Waste rice straw waste can now be easily converted into useful nanomaterials. Ammonium hydroxide was used as a passivation agent in a one-pot hydrothermal process for the manufacture of N-CDs from rice straw waste, which is a cheap and nontoxic method. N-CDs' probable formation mechanism was also examined. Fluorescence characteristics of the as-prepared N-CDs were good, with a QY as high as 22.51%. Scanning electron microscopy, X-ray fluorescence, fluorescence spectra, coloration screening and energy dispersive X-ray analysis were utilized to analyze the luminous security films. The printed security films showed an off-white color under visible daylight. Only the luminous layers produced a photochromic blue color under the UV lamp, which faded immediately after the ultraviolet lamp was removed. Only negligible changes were detected in the mechanical properties of the printed paper sheets as compared to the blank sheet. The ink's fluidity, perceived viscosity, stability and cytotoxicity were tested, demonstrating that the ink could be printed with ease without any toxic effects. Homogeneous dispersion of nano-scale carbon dots in the ink formulation ensured that the fluorescent layer was completely transparent. As a result of its quick reversibility, fatigue resistance and photostability, this colorless film can be applied under ambient conditions into a range of commercial surfaces for anti-counterfeiting reasons. It was monitored that the nitrogen-doped carbon dots are an efficient material for the purpose of storing and encrypting information as well as for marking paper sheets while retaining the solid surface's appearance, rigidity, and smoothness. Paper documents can be printed with the current security ink that is very durable, cheap and photostable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R122), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
Not applicable.
The author(s) agree to publish the article under the Creative Commons Attribution License.
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.
